expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to naming and writing formulas for hydrates—ionic compounds that incorporate water molecules into their crystal structure—using Greek prefixes to indicate water count and dot notation to separate water from the ionic compound.

core_concept.principle: Hydrates are ionic compounds with water molecules loosely bound in their crystal lattice; they are named by adding a Greek prefix + 'hydrate' to the ionic compound name, and formulas use a dot (·) to separate the compound from water molecules.
core_concept.explanation: Hydrates form when ionic compounds crystallize from aqueous solutions, trapping water molecules in their crystal structure. These water molecules are not chemically bonded to the ions but are held by intermolecular forces. The number of water molecules per formula unit is fixed and indicated by a Greek prefix in the name (mono-, di-, tri-, etc.) and by a subscript after a dot in the formula. For example, CuSO₄·5H₂O is copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, meaning each formula unit of copper(II) sulfate is associated with 5 water molecules. When heated, hydrates lose their water to become anhydrous compounds, often with dramatic color changes that make them useful as moisture indicators.

image.alt: Diagram showing hydrated vs. anhydrous copper(II) sulfate with color change
image.caption: Hydrates: ionic compounds with water in crystal structure; heating removes water to form anhydrous compound.

sections.introduction: Hydrates are fascinating compounds that demonstrate how water can be incorporated into solid ionic structures. Understanding hydrates is essential for laboratory work, industrial processes, and recognizing common household chemicals.

key_concept.definition: A hydrate is an ionic compound that has a specific number of water molecules associated with each formula unit in its crystal structure. The water molecules are loosely bound and can be removed by heating to form the anhydrous compound.
key_concept.context: Hydrates are written with a dot (·) separating the ionic compound from water molecules. The dot does NOT mean multiplication—it indicates water of hydration that is part of the crystal structure but not chemically bonded to the ions.

principles.statement: Water molecules in hydrates are held by intermolecular forces (ion-dipole, hydrogen bonding) in the crystal lattice, not by covalent or ionic bonds to the ions.
principles.application: Explains why heating removes water without decomposing the ionic compound; why hydrates often have different colors and properties than anhydrous forms.
principles.example: CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue) → CuSO₄ (white) + 5H₂O(g) upon heating. The blue color comes from water molecules coordinated to Cu²⁺ ions; removing water changes the coordination environment.
principles.analogy: Like guests at a party: water molecules are 'guests' in the ionic compound 'house'; heating asks the guests to leave, but the house (ionic compound) remains.

image.alt: Diagram showing water molecules in crystal lattice of hydrate
image.caption: Water of hydration: water molecules occupy spaces in ionic crystal lattice, held by intermolecular forces.

principles.statement: Each hydrate has a specific, fixed number of water molecules per formula unit; this ratio is indicated by the Greek prefix in the name and subscript in the formula.
principles.application: Enables precise formula writing and naming: CuSO₄·5H₂O always has exactly 5 water molecules per CuSO₄ unit; changing the number creates a different hydrate.
principles.example: Cobalt(II) chloride forms CoCl₂·6H₂O (pink, hexahydrate) and CoCl₂·2H₂O (violet, dihydrate)—different compounds with different properties.

image.alt: Comparison of different hydrates of same compound
image.caption: Fixed ratio: each hydrate has specific water count; different counts = different compounds.

principles.statement: The dot (·) in hydrate formulas separates the ionic compound from water molecules; it does NOT indicate a chemical bond or multiplication.
principles.application: Prevents misinterpretation: CuSO₄·5H₂O means 1 CuSO₄ unit + 5 H₂O molecules, NOT CuSO₄ × 5H₂O or a covalent bond between them.
principles.example: MgSO₄·7H₂O: 1 magnesium sulfate unit + 7 water molecules. The dot shows these are associated but distinct entities in the crystal.

image.alt: Diagram explaining dot notation in hydrate formulas
image.caption: Dot notation: · separates ionic compound from water molecules; not a bond or multiplication symbol.

components.equation: $\text{Hydrate Formula} = \text{Ionic Compound} \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{where } n = \text{number of water molecules}$
components.interpretation: Ionic compound formula first, then dot, then nH₂O. Name: ionic compound name + Greek prefix + 'hydrate'. Dot indicates association, not bonding.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Hydrates from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: For each formula, identify if it represents a hydrate and explain the meaning of the dot notation: (a) CuSO₄·5H₂O, (b) NaCl, (c) CaCl₂·2H₂O, (d) H₂SO₄.

step_by_step.calculation: Has ·5H₂O → hydrate. Means: 1 CuSO₄ unit + 5 H₂O molecules in crystal structure.
No dot + H₂O → not a hydrate. Simple ionic compound (sodium chloride).
Has ·2H₂O → hydrate. Means: 1 CaCl₂ unit + 2 H₂O molecules in crystal structure.
Has H and O but no dot + separate H₂O → not a hydrate. This is sulfuric acid (molecular compound).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CuSO₄·5H₂O: hydrate (5 water molecules per CuSO₄). (b) NaCl: not a hydrate. (c) CaCl₂·2H₂O: hydrate (2 water molecules per CaCl₂). (d) H₂SO₄: not a hydrate (this is an acid, not an ionic compound with water of hydration). Key: look for ·nH₂O pattern to identify hydrates.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Identifying hydrates by recognizing dot notation with water molecules

sections.introduction: Naming hydrates combines ionic compound nomenclature with Greek prefixes to indicate water count. The systematic approach ensures clear communication about which specific hydrate is being referenced.

key_concept.definition: To name a hydrate: (1) Name the ionic compound using standard rules (including Roman numerals if needed), (2) Add Greek prefix indicating number of water molecules, (3) Add the word 'hydrate'.
key_concept.context: The Greek prefix system is the same as for covalent compounds: mono(1), di(2), tri(3), tetra(4), penta(5), hexa(6), hepta(7), octa(8), nona(9), deca(10). The prefix modifies 'hydrate', not the ionic compound.

classifications.value: Fixed-charge metal ionic compound + hydrate prefix
classifications.behavior: Named: metal name + nonmetal-ide (or polyatomic name) + prefix + hydrate.

examples.explanation: CuSO₄ = copper(II) sulfate (Cu is Type II, needs Roman numeral). 5 H₂O = penta- + hydrate. Full name: copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. Blue crystals, common lab chemical.

image.caption: CuSO₄·5H₂O: copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (blue crystals).

examples.explanation: Na₂CO₃ = sodium carbonate (Na is Type I, no Roman numeral). 10 H₂O = deca- + hydrate. Full name: sodium carbonate decahydrate. Common name: washing soda.

image.caption: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O: sodium carbonate decahydrate (washing soda).

examples.explanation: MgSO₄ = magnesium sulfate (Mg is Type II but fixed +2, no Roman numeral needed). 7 H₂O = hepta- + hydrate. Full name: magnesium sulfate heptahydrate. Common name: Epsom salts.

image.caption: MgSO₄·7H₂O: magnesium sulfate heptahydrate (Epsom salts).

classifications.value: Variable-charge metal ionic compound + Roman numeral + hydrate prefix
characteristics: Often show color changes when dehydrated
classifications.behavior: Named: metal name + (Roman numeral) + nonmetal-ide (or polyatomic name) + prefix + hydrate.

examples.explanation: CoCl₂: Cl⁻ is -1, 2 Cl⁻ = -2, so Co = +2 → cobalt(II) chloride. 6 H₂O = hexa- + hydrate. Full name: cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate. Pink crystals; turns blue when anhydrous.

image.caption: CoCl₂·6H₂O: cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate (pink); anhydrous CoCl₂ is blue.

examples.explanation: FeCl₃: Cl⁻ is -1, 3 Cl⁻ = -3, so Fe = +3 → iron(III) chloride. 6 H₂O = hexa- + hydrate. Full name: iron(III) chloride hexahydrate. Yellow-orange crystals.

image.caption: FeCl₃·6H₂O: iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (yellow-orange).

classifications.value: Greek prefixes used for water molecule counts in hydrate names
characteristics: mono- rarely used (most hydrates have ≥2 H₂O)
classifications.behavior: Prefix indicates exact water count; must match subscript in formula.

examples.explanation: 1 = mono- (rare), 2 = di-, 3 = tri-, 4 = tetra-, 5 = penta-, 6 = hexa-, 7 = hepta-, 8 = octa-, 9 = nona-, 10 = deca-. Example: CaSO₄·2H₂O = calcium sulfate dihydrate (gypsum).

image.alt: Table of Greek prefixes for hydrate naming
image.caption: Hydrate prefixes: same as covalent compounds, modify 'hydrate'.

examples.explanation: Ba(OH)₂ = barium hydroxide (Ba is Type I). 8 H₂O = octa- + hydrate. Full name: barium hydroxide octahydrate. Used in titrations.

image.caption: Ba(OH)₂·8H₂O: barium hydroxide octahydrate.

principles.statement: Hydrate name = [Ionic compound name] + [Greek prefix] + hydrate. The prefix modifies only 'hydrate', not the ionic compound.
principles.application: CuSO₄·5H₂O: copper(II) sulfate (ionic name) + penta- (5) + hydrate = copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. NOT 'copper(II) pentasulfate hydrate'.
principles.example: Na₂S₂O₃·5H₂O: sodium thiosulfate (ionic) + penta- + hydrate = sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (photography chemical).

image.caption: Hydrate naming: ionic name + prefix + 'hydrate' (prefix modifies only 'hydrate').

principles.statement: Roman numerals for variable-charge metals appear in the ionic compound part of the name, BEFORE the hydrate prefix.
principles.application: FeSO₄·7H₂O: Fe²⁺ → iron(II) sulfate (Roman numeral in ionic part) + hepta- + hydrate = iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate.
principles.example: Correct: copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. Incorrect: copper sulfate(II) pentahydrate or copper sulfate pentahydrate(II). Roman numeral stays with metal name.

image.alt: Correct placement of Roman numerals in hydrate names
image.caption: Roman numerals: appear in ionic compound part, before hydrate prefix.

principles.statement: Unlike covalent compounds, hydrate names do NOT drop vowels before 'hydrate'; write the full prefix + 'hydrate'.
principles.application: CuSO₄·5H₂O: penta- + hydrate = pentahydrate (NOT 'pentahydrate' with dropped vowel—wait, actually we DO keep it: pentahydrate is correct). Clarification: the vowel dropping rule for 'oxide' does NOT apply to 'hydrate'.
principles.example: Correct: tetrahydrate (not 'tetrhydrate'), pentahydrate (not 'pentahydrate' with dropped vowel—actually both are accepted, but standard is to keep the vowel: tetrahydrate, pentahydrate).

image.alt: Hydrate naming: no vowel dropping required
image.caption: Hydrate naming: write full prefix + 'hydrate' (no vowel dropping rule).

components.interpretation: Ionic compound named first (with Roman numeral if Type II), then Greek prefix for water count, then 'hydrate'. Prefix modifies only 'hydrate'.

worked_examples.title: Naming Hydrates from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following hydrates: (a) CaCl₂·2H₂O, (b) Na₃PO₄·12H₂O, (c) CoCl₂·6H₂O, (d) AlCl₃·6H₂O.

step_by_step.calculation: CaCl₂ = calcium chloride (Ca is Type I, no Roman numeral). 2 H₂O = di- + hydrate. Name: calcium chloride dihydrate.
Na₃PO₄ = sodium phosphate (Na is Type I). 12 H₂O = dodeca- + hydrate. Name: sodium phosphate dodecahydrate.
CoCl₂: 2 Cl⁻ = -2, so Co = +2 → cobalt(II) chloride. 6 H₂O = hexa- + hydrate. Name: cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate.
AlCl₃ = aluminum chloride (Al is Type I, fixed +3). 6 H₂O = hexa- + hydrate. Name: aluminum chloride hexahydrate.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CaCl₂·2H₂O: calcium chloride dihydrate. (b) Na₃PO₄·12H₂O: sodium phosphate dodecahydrate. (c) CoCl₂·6H₂O: cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate. (d) AlCl₃·6H₂O: aluminum chloride hexahydrate. All follow pattern: ionic name + prefix + 'hydrate'.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming hydrates using ionic nomenclature + Greek prefix for water count

worked_examples.title: Complex Hydrate Naming Practice
worked_examples.problem: Name these more complex hydrates: (a) Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O, (b) KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O, (c) Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O.

step_by_step.calculation: Ionic part: Fe²⁺ + 2NH₄⁺ + 2SO₄²⁻ = iron(II) ammonium sulfate. 6 H₂O = hexahydrate. Name: iron(II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate (Mohr's salt).
Ionic part: K⁺ + Al³⁺ + 2SO₄²⁻ = potassium aluminum sulfate. 12 H₂O = dodecahydrate. Name: potassium aluminum sulfate dodecahydrate (alum).
Ionic part: Na₂B₄O₇ = sodium tetraborate. 10 H₂O = decahydrate. Name: sodium tetraborate decahydrate (common name: borax).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O: iron(II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate. (b) KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O: potassium aluminum sulfate dodecahydrate. (c) Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O: sodium tetraborate decahydrate. Complex hydrates follow same naming pattern; name ionic part first, then add prefix + 'hydrate'.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming complex hydrates with polyatomic ions and multiple cations

sections.introduction: Writing formulas for hydrates requires combining the ionic compound formula with the correct number of water molecules using dot notation. This skill is essential for laboratory work and chemical calculations.

key_concept.definition: To write a hydrate formula: (1) Write the ionic compound formula using standard rules, (2) Add a dot (·), (3) Write the number of water molecules as a subscript before H₂O.
key_concept.context: The dot is crucial—it shows water is associated with but not bonded to the ionic compound. The subscript applies only to H₂O, not to the entire formula.

principles.statement: Use a centered dot (·) to separate the ionic compound formula from water molecules; never use a period (.) or multiplication sign (×).
principles.application: Correct: CuSO₄·5H₂O. Incorrect: CuSO₄.5H₂O (period) or CuSO₄×5H₂O (multiplication). The dot is a specific notation for hydration.
principles.example: MgSO₄·7H₂O: the dot shows 7 H₂O molecules are associated with each MgSO₄ unit in the crystal, not chemically bonded.

image.alt: Correct vs. incorrect dot notation in hydrate formulas
image.caption: Dot notation: use centered dot (·), not period (.) or multiplication sign (×).

principles.statement: The subscript for water molecules appears immediately before H₂O, not after; it applies only to the water, not to the ionic compound.
principles.application: CuSO₄·5H₂O means 1 CuSO₄ + 5 H₂O, NOT (CuSO₄·5)H₂O or CuSO₄·(5H)₂O. The subscript 5 applies only to H₂O.
principles.example: CaCl₂·2H₂O: 1 CaCl₂ unit + 2 H₂O molecules. The '2' applies only to H₂O, not to CaCl₂.

image.alt: Correct subscript placement in hydrate formulas
image.caption: Subscript placement: number before H₂O applies only to water molecules.

principles.statement: Use parentheses around polyatomic ions in the ionic compound part when needed; do NOT put parentheses around the entire hydrate or around H₂O.
principles.application: Correct: Ca(OH)₂·2H₂O (parentheses around OH because subscript 2 applies to OH). Incorrect: Ca(OH)₂·(2H₂O) or (Ca(OH)₂·2H₂O).
principles.example: Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O: parentheses around SO₄ because subscript 3 applies to sulfate; no parentheses around 18H₂O.

image.alt: Correct parentheses usage in hydrate formulas
image.caption: Parentheses: use around polyatomic ions in ionic part; never around water molecules.

components.interpretation: Write ionic formula first (with parentheses if needed), add centered dot, then nH₂O with subscript n. Dot indicates association, not bonding.

worked_examples.title: Writing Hydrate Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for the following hydrates: (a) barium chloride dihydrate, (b) sodium sulfate decahydrate, (c) cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate, (d) magnesium sulfate heptahydrate.

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for barium chloride dihydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Barium chloride: Ba²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → BaCl₂. Dihydrate: 2 H₂O. Formula: BaCl₂·2H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for sodium sulfate decahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Sodium sulfate: 2Na⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → Na₂SO₄. Decahydrate: 10 H₂O. Formula: Na₂SO₄·10H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Cobalt(II) chloride: Co²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → CoCl₂. Hexahydrate: 6 H₂O. Formula: CoCl₂·6H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for magnesium sulfate heptahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Magnesium sulfate: Mg²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → MgSO₄. Heptahydrate: 7 H₂O. Formula: MgSO₄·7H₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) BaCl₂·2H₂O, (b) Na₂SO₄·10H₂O, (c) CoCl₂·6H₂O, (d) MgSO₄·7H₂O. All use centered dot (·) to separate ionic compound from water; subscript indicates water count.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing hydrate formulas using ionic compound rules + dot notation + water subscript

worked_examples.title: Complex Hydrate Formula Writing
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) iron(II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate, (b) potassium aluminum sulfate dodecahydrate, (c) sodium tetraborate decahydrate.

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for iron(II) ammonium sulfate hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Ionic part: Fe²⁺ + 2NH₄⁺ + 2SO₄²⁻ → Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂. Hexahydrate: 6 H₂O. Formula: Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for potassium aluminum sulfate dodecahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Ionic part: K⁺ + Al³⁺ + 2SO₄²⁻ → KAl(SO₄)₂. Dodecahydrate: 12 H₂O. Formula: KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for sodium tetraborate decahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Ionic part: Na₂B₄O₇. Decahydrate: 10 H₂O. Formula: Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Fe(NH₄)₂(SO₄)₂·6H₂O, (b) KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O, (c) Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O. Complex hydrates follow same pattern: write ionic formula (with parentheses as needed), add dot, then nH₂O.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing formulas for complex hydrates with polyatomic ions

sections.introduction: Hydrates have unique properties and important applications in chemistry, industry, and daily life. Understanding these properties helps explain why hydrates matter beyond nomenclature.

key_concept.definition: Hydrates often have different physical properties (color, solubility, density) than their anhydrous forms; heating removes water to form anhydrous compounds, a process used in moisture detection, chemical synthesis, and industrial processes.
key_concept.context: The reversible hydration/dehydration of some compounds makes them useful as desiccants (drying agents), humidity indicators, and in thermal energy storage.

classifications.value: Many hydrates change color when water is removed, making them useful as moisture indicators
classifications.behavior: Water molecules coordinated to metal ions affect d-orbital splitting, changing light absorption and color.

examples.explanation: CoCl₂·6H₂O (pink) ⇌ CoCl₂ (blue) + 6H₂O. Pink when hydrated (moist), blue when anhydrous (dry). Used in humidity indicator cards and silica gel desiccants.

image.alt: Cobalt chloride color change from pink to blue
image.caption: CoCl₂·6H₂O: pink when hydrated, blue when anhydrous—visual moisture indicator.

examples.explanation: CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue) → CuSO₄ (white) + 5H₂O(g). Classic lab demonstration; blue color returns when water is added to white anhydrous CuSO₄.

image.caption: CuSO₄·5H₂O: blue hydrate → white anhydrous + water vapor; reversible.

classifications.value: Some anhydrous compounds readily absorb water to form hydrates, making them effective drying agents
classifications.behavior: Anhydrous compounds with strong affinity for water remove moisture from gases, solvents, or enclosed spaces.

examples.explanation: Anhydrous CaCl₂ absorbs water to form CaCl₂·2H₂O or CaCl₂·6H₂O. Used to dry gases, in dehumidifiers, and on roads to absorb moisture and prevent ice.

image.caption: CaCl₂: anhydrous form absorbs water to form hydrates—effective desiccant.

examples.explanation: Silica gel (SiO₂) often contains CoCl₂ indicator: blue when dry (anhydrous CoCl₂), pink when wet (CoCl₂·6H₂O). Regenerated by heating to drive off water.

image.alt: Silica gel with cobalt chloride indicator
image.caption: Silica gel + CoCl₂: blue = dry, pink = wet—visual moisture indicator.

classifications.value: Hydrates are used in numerous practical applications due to their stability, solubility, or thermal properties
classifications.behavior: Hydrates provide convenient solid forms of ionic compounds with predictable water content for industrial processes.

examples.explanation: Magnesium sulfate heptahydrate used in baths for muscle relaxation, in agriculture as magnesium source, and in medicine as laxative. Hydrate form is stable and soluble.

image.caption: MgSO₄·7H₂O: Epsom salts—hydrate form is stable, soluble, and useful.

examples.explanation: Sodium carbonate decahydrate used in laundry detergents, water softening, and glass manufacturing. Decahydrate form is easier to handle than anhydrous powder.

image.caption: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O: washing soda—hydrate form convenient for household use.

examples.explanation: Sodium tetraborate decahydrate used in laundry boosters, cleaning products, and as flux in metalwork. Hydrate form provides controlled boron release.

image.caption: Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O: borax—hydrate form useful in cleaning and metallurgy.

principles.statement: Heating a hydrate drives off water molecules to form the anhydrous compound; the reaction is often reversible by adding water.
principles.application: Used to determine water content in hydrates (gravimetric analysis); to regenerate desiccants; to prepare anhydrous compounds for synthesis.
principles.example: CuSO₄·5H₂O(s) → CuSO₄(s) + 5H₂O(g) upon heating. Mass loss = mass of water; used to calculate % water in hydrate.

image.caption: Dehydration: heating hydrate → anhydrous compound + water vapor; often reversible.

principles.statement: The mass percent of water in a hydrate can be calculated from its formula: %H₂O = (mass of nH₂O / molar mass of hydrate) × 100%.
principles.application: Used to verify hydrate formula experimentally; to calculate amounts of anhydrous compound needed for reactions; to determine hydration state.
principles.example: CuSO₄·5H₂O: molar mass = 249.7 g/mol; mass of 5H₂O = 90.1 g; %H₂O = (90.1/249.7)×100% = 36.1%. Heating 10.0 g hydrate yields 6.39 g anhydrous CuSO₄.

image.alt: Percent water calculation example for copper sulfate pentahydrate
image.caption: Percent water: (mass of nH₂O / molar mass of hydrate) × 100%.

components.interpretation: n = number of water molecules; M = molar mass. Percent water useful for experimental verification of hydrate formula.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Percent Water in a Hydrate
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the mass percent of water in copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, CuSO₄·5H₂O. Molar masses: Cu = 63.55, S = 32.07, O = 16.00, H = 1.008 g/mol.
worked_examples.final_answer: Copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate is 36.08% water by mass. This means heating 100.0 g of CuSO₄·5H₂O would yield 63.92 g of anhydrous CuSO₄ and release 36.08 g of water vapor. Percent water calculations are essential for hydrate analysis.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Calculating mass percent of water in hydrates using molar masses

worked_examples.title: Determining Hydrate Formula from Experimental Data
worked_examples.problem: A 2.50 g sample of a hydrate of magnesium sulfate is heated, leaving 1.22 g of anhydrous MgSO₄. Determine the formula of the hydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: Mass H₂O = 2.50 g (hydrate) - 1.22 g (anhydrous) = 1.28 g H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Find mole ratio of H₂O to MgSO₄.
step_by_step.calculation: Ratio = 0.07105 mol H₂O ÷ 0.01014 mol MgSO₄ = 7.01 ≈ 7
Ratio ≈ 7:1 → MgSO₄·7H₂O (magnesium sulfate heptahydrate)

worked_examples.final_answer: The hydrate formula is MgSO₄·7H₂O (magnesium sulfate heptahydrate, Epsom salts). Experimental determination: mass loss on heating gives water mass; mole ratio gives water count per formula unit.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Determining hydrate formula from experimental dehydration data

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Hydrate nomenclature and formula writing have specific conventions that differ from other compound types. Recognizing common pitfalls prevents errors in laboratory work and chemical communication.

key_concept.definition: Common hydrate errors include: using wrong Greek prefix, misplacing the dot, adding parentheses around water, forgetting Roman numerals in ionic part, and confusing hydrates with other water-containing compounds.
key_concept.context: A verification checklist ensures accuracy: ionic compound named correctly? Greek prefix matches water count? Dot notation used? No parentheses around H₂O? Roman numeral if Type II metal?

classifications.type: Wrong Greek Prefix for Water Count
classifications.value: Using incorrect prefix that doesn't match the number of water molecules
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect identification of compound; different hydrates have different properties and uses.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'calcium chloride trihydrate' for CaCl₂·2H₂O. Correct: 'calcium chloride dihydrate'. Prefix must match subscript: di- = 2, tri- = 3.

image.alt: Comparison showing correct vs. incorrect hydrate prefixes
image.caption: Hydrate error: prefix must match water count (di- = 2, tri- = 3, etc.).

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'copper(II) sulfate hexahydrate' for CuSO₄·5H₂O. Correct: 'copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate'. Penta- = 5, hexa- = 6.

image.caption: CuSO₄·5H₂O: pentahydrate (5), not hexahydrate (6).

classifications.value: Using period, multiplication sign, or no separator instead of centered dot
classifications.behavior: Results in ambiguous or incorrect formulas; dot notation is standardized for hydrates.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: CuSO₄.5H₂O (period). Correct: CuSO₄·5H₂O (centered dot). Period suggests decimal; dot indicates hydration.

image.caption: Dot notation: use centered dot (·), not period (.) or other symbols.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: MgSO₄7H₂O (no separator). Correct: MgSO₄·7H₂O. Without dot, formula is ambiguous and incorrect.

image.caption: Always use dot (·) to separate ionic compound from water molecules.

classifications.value: Incorrectly adding parentheses around water molecules in formula
characteristics: Confusing hydrate notation with polyatomic ions
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect formulas; parentheses are only for polyatomic ions in the ionic compound part, not for water.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: CaCl₂·(2H₂O). Correct: CaCl₂·2H₂O. Parentheses are not used around water molecules in hydrate formulas.

image.alt: Incorrect parentheses around water in hydrate formula
image.caption: Hydrate formulas: no parentheses around water molecules.

classifications.value: Mistaking acids, molecular compounds, or solutions for hydrates
characteristics: Calling H₂SO₄ a hydrate (it's an acid)
Calling CuSO₄(aq) a hydrate (it's a solution)
Confusing water of hydration with solvent water
classifications.behavior: Results in incorrect classification and naming; hydrates are solid ionic compounds with water in crystal lattice, not solutions or molecular compounds.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: 'sulfuric acid hydrate' for H₂SO₄. Correct: H₂SO₄ is sulfuric acid (molecular compound), not a hydrate. Hydrates are ionic compounds with water in crystal structure.

image.caption: Acids (H₂SO₄) are not hydrates; hydrates are ionic compounds with water in crystal lattice.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: calling CuSO₄(aq) 'copper sulfate hydrate'. Correct: CuSO₄(aq) is a solution; CuSO₄·5H₂O is the solid hydrate. Solutions have water as solvent; hydrates have water in crystal structure.

image.caption: Solutions (CuSO₄(aq)) ≠ hydrates (CuSO₄·5H₂O); different states and structures.

principles.statement: Always verify hydrate names/formulas by checking: (1) Ionic compound named correctly (Roman numeral if Type II), (2) Greek prefix matches water count, (3) Centered dot (·) used, (4) No parentheses around H₂O, (5) Subscript applies only to H₂O.
principles.application: Prevents common errors; systematic verification builds accuracy habit for laboratory work and chemical communication.
principles.example: Name: copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. Check: CuSO₄ = copper(II) sulfate ✓, penta- = 5 ✓, formula CuSO₄·5H₂O ✓, dot used ✓, no parentheses around 5H₂O ✓. All checks pass.

image.caption: Hydrate checklist: ionic name correct, prefix matches water count, dot notation, no parentheses around H₂O.

principles.statement: Always specify whether a compound is hydrated or anhydrous in names and formulas; they are different substances with different properties.
principles.application: Prevents confusion in laboratory work: CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue, hydrate) vs. CuSO₄ (white, anhydrous) have different masses, solubilities, and uses.
principles.example: Ordering 'copper sulfate' without specifying hydrate state could yield either CuSO₄·5H₂O or CuSO₄—very different for experiments. Always specify: 'copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate' or 'anhydrous copper(II) sulfate'.

image.alt: Comparison of hydrate and anhydrous forms
image.caption: Hydrate vs. anhydrous: different compounds with different properties; always specify which form.

worked_examples.title: Error Identification and Correction
worked_examples.problem: Identify the error in each name or formula and provide the correct version: (a) 'copper sulfate pentahydrate' for CuSO₄·5H₂O, (b) CaCl₂.(2H₂O) for calcium chloride dihydrate, (c) 'magnesium sulfate heptahydrate' for MgSO₄·6H₂O, (d) Na₂CO₃·10H₂O written as Na₂CO₃10H₂O.

step_by_step.calculation: Error: missing Roman numeral for Type II metal. Correction: Cu is variable charge; in CuSO₄, SO₄²⁻ = -2, so Cu = +2. Correct name: copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze CaCl₂.(2H₂O) for calcium chloride dihydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Error: period instead of centered dot AND parentheses around water. Correction: Use centered dot, remove parentheses. Correct formula: CaCl₂·2H₂O.
Error: prefix doesn't match water count. Hepta- = 7, but formula has 6 H₂O. Correction: hexa- = 6. Correct name: magnesium sulfate hexahydrate.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze Na₂CO₃10H₂O for sodium carbonate decahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Error: missing dot separator. Correction: Add centered dot between ionic compound and water. Correct formula: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 'copper sulfate pentahydrate' → copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (add Roman numeral for Cu²⁺). (b) CaCl₂.(2H₂O) → CaCl₂·2H₂O (use centered dot, remove parentheses). (c) 'magnesium sulfate heptahydrate' for MgSO₄·6H₂O → magnesium sulfate hexahydrate (hexa- = 6, not hepta- = 7). (d) Na₂CO₃10H₂O → Na₂CO₃·10H₂O (add centered dot). Systematic verification catches these common hydrate errors.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction using hydrate nomenclature verification checklist

sections.introduction: Hydrate nomenclature mastery requires active practice with naming, formula writing, and calculations. Work through these problems to build confidence and accuracy with hydrate conventions.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems cover: naming hydrates from formulas, writing formulas from names, calculating percent water, and correcting common errors.
key_concept.context: Active recall and immediate feedback reinforce Greek prefix memorization and dot notation usage; focus on distinguishing hydrates from other compound types.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Hydrate Practice Set
worked_examples.problem: Complete the following: (1-5) Name these hydrates: Ba(OH)₂·8H₂O, Na₂S₂O₃·5H₂O, FeSO₄·7H₂O, CoCl₂·6H₂O, Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O. (6-10) Write formulas for: calcium sulfate dihydrate, sodium phosphate dodecahydrate, nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate, potassium carbonate sesquihydrate (1.5 H₂O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: Ba(OH)₂ = barium hydroxide (Ba is Type I). 8 H₂O = octa- + hydrate. Name: barium hydroxide octahydrate.
Na₂S₂O₃ = sodium thiosulfate. 5 H₂O = penta- + hydrate. Name: sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate.
FeSO₄: SO₄²⁻ = -2, so Fe = +2 → iron(II) sulfate. 7 H₂O = hepta- + hydrate. Name: iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate.
CoCl₂: 2 Cl⁻ = -2, so Co = +2 → cobalt(II) chloride. 6 H₂O = hexa- + hydrate. Name: cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate.
Al(NO₃)₃ = aluminum nitrate (Al is Type I). 9 H₂O = nona- + hydrate. Name: aluminum nitrate nonahydrate.

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for calcium sulfate dihydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Calcium sulfate: Ca²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → CaSO₄. Dihydrate: 2 H₂O. Formula: CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum).

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for sodium phosphate dodecahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Sodium phosphate: 3Na⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → Na₃PO₄. Dodecahydrate: 12 H₂O. Formula: Na₃PO₄·12H₂O.

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Nickel(II) chloride: Ni²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → NiCl₂. Hexahydrate: 6 H₂O. Formula: NiCl₂·6H₂O.

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for potassium carbonate sesquihydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Potassium carbonate: 2K⁺ + CO₃²⁻ → K₂CO₃. Sesquihydrate: 1.5 H₂O = 3/2 H₂O. Formula: K₂CO₃·1.5H₂O or K₂CO₃·³/₂H₂O (often written as K₂CO₃·1.5H₂O).

step_by_step.explanation: Write formula for zinc nitrate hexahydrate.
step_by_step.calculation: Zinc nitrate: Zn²⁺ + 2NO₃⁻ → Zn(NO₃)₂. Hexahydrate: 6 H₂O. Formula: Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O.

worked_examples.final_answer: Names: (1) barium hydroxide octahydrate, (2) sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate, (3) iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate, (4) cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate, (5) aluminum nitrate nonahydrate. Formulas: (6) CaSO₄·2H₂O, (7) Na₃PO₄·12H₂O, (8) NiCl₂·6H₂O, (9) K₂CO₃·1.5H₂O, (10) Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O. All use centered dot (·), correct Greek prefixes, and proper ionic compound naming.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive hydrate nomenclature practice covering naming, formula writing, and special cases

worked_examples.title: Hydrate Identification and Percent Water Calculation
worked_examples.problem: A 3.44 g sample of a hydrate of nickel(II) chloride is heated, leaving 1.88 g of anhydrous NiCl₂. (a) Determine the formula of the hydrate. (b) Calculate the mass percent of water in the hydrate.

step_by_step.calculation: Mass H₂O = 3.44 g (hydrate) - 1.88 g (anhydrous) = 1.56 g H₂O

step_by_step.explanation: Find mole ratio of H₂O to NiCl₂.
step_by_step.calculation: Ratio = 0.08659 mol H₂O ÷ 0.01451 mol NiCl₂ = 5.97 ≈ 6

step_by_step.explanation: Write hydrate formula and calculate percent water.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Formula: NiCl₂·6H₂O (nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate). (b) Percent water: 45.35%. Experimental determination: mass loss on heating gives water mass; mole ratio gives water count; percent water = (mass H₂O / mass hydrate) × 100%.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Determining hydrate formula and calculating percent water from experimental data

summary: Hydrates are ionic compounds with water molecules incorporated into their crystal structure, named by adding Greek prefix + 'hydrate' to the ionic compound name and written with dot notation: [Ionic Compound]·nH₂O. Naming: name ionic compound first (with Roman numeral if Type II metal), then Greek prefix for water count, then 'hydrate' (e.g., CuSO₄·5H₂O = copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate). Formula writing: write ionic formula, add centered dot (·), then nH₂O with subscript n (e.g., calcium chloride dihydrate = CaCl₂·2H₂O). Dot notation: · indicates association, not chemical bond or multiplication; never use period (.) or parentheses around H₂O. Common hydrates: CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue, copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate), MgSO₄·7H₂O (Epsom salts), Na₂CO₃·10H₂O (washing soda), CoCl₂·6H₂O (pink, moisture indicator). Properties: hydrates often have different colors/solubilities than anhydrous forms; heating removes water (dehydration) to form anhydrous compound, often reversibly. Applications: moisture indicators (CoCl₂), desiccants (CaCl₂), household products (Epsom salts, washing soda), laboratory reagents. Calculations: %H₂O = (mass of nH₂O / molar mass of hydrate) × 100%; experimental determination from mass loss on heating. Common errors: wrong Greek prefix, incorrect dot notation, parentheses around water, missing Roman numerals, confusing hydrates with acids/solutions. Verification checklist: ionic name correct? Prefix matches water count? Centered dot used? No parentheses around H₂O? Roman numeral if Type II? Mastery of hydrate nomenclature essential for laboratory work and chemical communication.
